content.text: Preload: filling, stretch and pressure before contraction

Preload is the stretch on myocardial fibres at the end of diastole, immediately before contraction. At the bedside, we cannot directly measure myocardial fibre stretch, so we estimate preload using pressure, volume appearance and response. For the left ventricle, preload is approximated by LV end-diastolic volume, LV end-diastolic pressure, PAWP or PCWP if a pulmonary artery catheter is present, and echocardiographic assessment of LV filling. For the right ventricle, preload is commonly approximated by CVP or right atrial pressure.

content.definition: Preload is ventricular myocardial stretch at end-diastole. Clinically, it refers to the filling condition of the ventricle before systole. It is related to end-diastolic volume and pressure, but pressure is only an indirect estimate because compliance strongly affects the number.

LVEDP means left ventricular end-diastolic pressure. It is the pressure in the left ventricle just before systole. It reflects both LV volume and LV compliance. A stiff LV can have a high LVEDP with only modest volume.

CVP means central venous pressure. It reflects pressure in the thoracic vena cava or right atrium and is used as a right-sided filling pressure estimate. It is not a direct measure of circulating volume.

content.text: The most important preload rule in CT-ICU is that filling pressure is not the same as fluid volume. CVP is a pressure, not a tank gauge. A CVP of 14 mmHg may occur because the patient is full, but it may also occur because the RV is failing, pulmonary vascular resistance is high, tamponade is restricting filling, PEEP is high, intrathoracic pressure is elevated, a tension pneumothorax is present, the tricuspid valve is regurgitant or venous congestion is severe. A CVP of 4 mmHg may suggest low right-sided filling pressure, but it does not automatically prove the patient needs fluid.

Preload matters because of the Frank-Starling mechanism. If the ventricle is on the responsive part of the curve, extra filling increases myocardial stretch and improves stroke volume. If the ventricle is already full, stiff, compressed or failing, extra filling mostly raises pressure rather than flow. In that situation, fluid can worsen pulmonary oedema, RV dilation, hepatic congestion, renal venous congestion and oxygenation.

After cardiac surgery, preload interpretation is difficult because many factors distort pressure measurements. Cardiopulmonary bypass can cause myocardial oedema and reduced compliance. Longstanding valve disease can leave the ventricle hypertrophied or dilated. Positive pressure ventilation and PEEP increase intrathoracic pressure and affect venous return. RV dysfunction can raise CVP while reducing LV preload. Tamponade can raise filling pressures while preventing effective filling.

The bedside question is not, “What is the CVP?” The safer question is, “Will more filling increase stroke volume, and can this patient tolerate the extra volume?” Fluid responsiveness means stroke volume or cardiac output rises after a preload challenge. Fluid tolerance means the patient can accept that fluid without harmful congestion. A patient can be fluid responsive but not fluid tolerant, especially with RV failure, pulmonary hypertension or a stiff LV.

In a fresh post-operative cardiac patient, falling MAP with low or falling CVP, bleeding drains, tachycardia and narrowing pulse pressure may suggest hypovolaemia or haemorrhage. Falling MAP with rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure, oliguria and rising lactate is much more concerning for tamponade, RV failure, high PVR, excessive PEEP or tension physiology. The pattern determines the urgency and the response.

Preload is about useful filling, not filling pressure alone. CVP and PAWP are clues, not instructions. In CT-ICU, fluid is a physiological test: it should improve stroke volume, cardiac index, pulse pressure, urine output, lactate trend or venous saturation without causing harmful rises in CVP, PAWP, PA pressure, pulmonary oedema, RV dilation or renal venous congestion.
Preload as end-diastolic myocardial stretch

Preload is one of the core determinants of stroke volume. Physiologically, it is the stretch of myocardial fibres at the end of diastole, immediately before systole. That stretch is related to ventricular end-diastolic volume, but in clinical practice we often estimate it from pressures such as CVP, PAWP or LVEDP because direct measurement of ventricular fibre stretch is not possible at the bedside.

The reason preload matters is the Frank-Starling mechanism. Within a useful physiological range, increased ventricular filling stretches myocardial fibres and improves contraction force, increasing stroke volume. This is the mechanism behind fluid responsiveness. However, once the ventricle reaches the shoulder or flat part of the Starling curve, additional volume produces little increase in stroke volume and a disproportionate increase in filling pressure. In CT-ICU, this is where fluid becomes harmful.

Preload must therefore be interpreted as a relationship between volume, pressure, compliance and output. Volume is how much blood is present. Pressure is what that volume produces inside the chamber. Compliance is how easily the chamber accepts volume. Output is whether the added filling produces useful forward flow. A compliant ventricle accepts volume with little rise in pressure. A stiff ventricle develops high pressure with smaller volume changes. This is why the same PAWP or CVP can mean different things in different patients.
LVEDP: left-sided filling pressure

LVEDP is left ventricular end-diastolic pressure. It is the pressure inside the LV at the end of filling, immediately before ventricular contraction. LVEDP is influenced by LV volume and LV compliance. If the LV is compliant, it can accept a reasonable volume without a large pressure rise. If the LV is stiff, even a moderate volume can produce a high LVEDP.

This is a common issue in cardiothoracic ICU. A patient with longstanding aortic stenosis may have concentric LV hypertrophy and poor compliance. After valve replacement, the obstruction is relieved, but the LV does not instantly become compliant. The patient may require adequate preload to generate stroke volume, but excessive volume can rapidly raise LVEDP and cause pulmonary oedema. A patient with chronic hypertension or diastolic dysfunction behaves similarly.

Cardiopulmonary bypass can also impair LV compliance. Myocardial oedema, reperfusion injury, hypothermia, inflammation and stunning can make the ventricle less able to relax. The ventricle may be mechanically full at a lower volume than expected. Fluid loading such a ventricle may raise PAWP, worsen oxygenation and increase work of breathing after extubation without improving cardiac output.
PAWP or PCWP as a left-sided preload estimate

When a pulmonary artery catheter is present, PAWP or PCWP may be used as an estimate of left atrial pressure and left-sided filling pressure. The catheter balloon is inflated in a branch of the pulmonary artery, creating a static column of blood between the catheter tip and the left atrium. If the measurement is valid, the wedge pressure approximates downstream pulmonary venous and left atrial pressure.

PAWP is not automatically LV preload. It is a pressure estimate and must be interpreted carefully. It can be misleading with incorrect catheter position, overwedging, underwedging, severe mitral regurgitation, high PEEP, poor lung zone conditions, pulmonary venous obstruction or large respiratory swings. PAWP also reflects pressure, not volume. A stiff LV can have a high PAWP without being massively volume loaded. A vasodilated patient can have a low PAWP and still be globally fluid overloaded in the tissues.

In CT-ICU, PAWP is most useful when interpreted with cardiac index, CVP, PA pressures, SvO2, oxygenation, lung mechanics and echo. A PAWP of 18 mmHg with pulmonary oedema, low cardiac index and poor LV contraction suggests left-sided congestion and pump failure. A PAWP of 8 mmHg with low CVP, narrow pulse pressure, bleeding drains and fluid responsiveness suggests underfilling. A PAWP of 14 mmHg with low cardiac index and rising CVP may still be inadequate LV preload if RV failure is limiting pulmonary flow, but the pattern must be confirmed.
CVP: right-sided pressure, not right-sided volume

CVP is central venous pressure, usually measured from the superior vena cava or right atrium. It is commonly used as a right-sided filling pressure estimate. A typical reference range is often 5 to 12 mmHg, but this range is not a treatment target by itself. CVP is affected by right atrial pressure, venous return, RV compliance, RV systolic function, tricuspid valve function, pulmonary vascular resistance, intrathoracic pressure, PEEP, abdominal pressure and pericardial pressure.

A low CVP can occur with hypovolaemia, vasodilation, reduced stressed volume or venous pooling. A high CVP can occur with volume loading, RV failure, high PVR, tamponade, high PEEP, tension pneumothorax, severe tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary embolism, raised intra-abdominal pressure or venous congestion. Therefore, CVP must be read as part of a pattern. It is more useful as a trend than as an isolated value.

The common novice error is to treat CVP as an intravascular fuel gauge. It is not. A CVP of 3 mmHg does not automatically mandate fluid, and a CVP of 16 mmHg does not automatically mean fluid overload. The correct question is whether the right heart is adequately filled, whether it can eject, whether the LV is being filled, whether tissue perfusion is adequate, and whether adding fluid would improve stroke volume without causing harm.
Venous return and stressed volume

Venous return is driven by the pressure gradient between the systemic venous reservoir and the right atrium. Only part of the blood volume creates pressure that drives venous return. This portion is called stressed volume. The rest sits in the venous capacitance system as unstressed volume. Vasodilation increases venous capacitance, shifting blood into unstressed volume and reducing effective venous return even if total body blood volume has not changed dramatically.

After cardiopulmonary bypass, vasoplegia can reduce venous tone and stressed volume. A patient may have low effective preload because the venous system is too dilated, not because the absolute blood volume is profoundly low. In that situation, vasopressors can sometimes improve preload by recruiting unstressed volume into stressed volume and restoring venous return. Fluid may help if the patient is fluid responsive, but repeated fluid without stroke volume response can worsen oedema and oxygenation.
Positive pressure ventilation and preload

Positive pressure ventilation changes preload physiology. During spontaneous breathing, negative intrathoracic pressure tends to support venous return. During positive pressure ventilation, intrathoracic pressure rises during inspiration. This can reduce the pressure gradient for venous return and increase measured right-sided pressures. PEEP maintains positive pressure at end-expiration and may further reduce venous return, especially if intravascular volume is low or RV function is poor.

PEEP can also increase RV afterload if it causes alveolar overdistension and compression of pulmonary vessels. This creates a double hit: venous return can fall, and the RV may face higher afterload. In an RV-risk patient, increasing PEEP may raise CVP, reduce cardiac output and worsen hypotension. Conversely, appropriate PEEP can improve oxygenation and reduce hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction. The haemodynamic effect depends on lung mechanics, volume status, RV function, PVR and oxygenation response.

This is why filling pressures in ventilated patients need context. A CVP of 12 mmHg on high PEEP may not represent the same transmural cardiac filling as a CVP of 12 mmHg in a spontaneously breathing patient. When numbers do not fit the patient, look at airway pressures, PEEP, respiratory variation, echo and response to ventilator changes.
Preload in RV failure

RV failure is one of the most important preload traps. The CVP may be high because the RV cannot eject forward. The LV may be underfilled because the RV is failing to deliver blood through the pulmonary circulation. This means the patient can have high right-sided filling pressure and low left-sided preload at the same time.

Giving fluid in RV failure can be harmful. Extra volume may dilate the RV further, worsen tricuspid regurgitation, shift the septum toward the LV, reduce LV end-diastolic volume, increase CVP, worsen hepatic congestion and reduce renal perfusion by increasing renal venous pressure. The patient may become more hypotensive despite higher CVP. This is why RV failure is often managed by optimising oxygenation, lowering PVR, correcting hypercapnia and acidosis, supporting RV contractility, maintaining systemic pressure for RV coronary perfusion and using cautious preload rather than blind fluid loading.
Preload in tamponade

Tamponade is an external filling problem. Blood or clot in the pericardial space raises external pressure around the heart and restricts diastolic filling. The ventricles cannot expand normally. Stroke volume falls. Venous pressure rises. Pulse pressure narrows. MAP falls. Tachycardia develops as compensation. In a ventilated post-cardiac surgery patient, classic signs such as muffled heart sounds or pulsus paradoxus may be absent or difficult to detect.

A dangerous post-operative pattern is falling MAP, rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure, falling urine output and rising lactate. Chest drain output may be high if bleeding is freely draining, but drain output may also be low if clot blocks the drains and blood accumulates around the heart. Low drain output does not exclude tamponade. In this pattern, repeated fluid may briefly support pressure, but it does not correct the external compression. Urgent escalation and echo or surgical assessment are required.
Preload in hypovolaemia and bleeding

Hypovolaemia reduces venous return and ventricular filling. In early bleeding, CVP and PAWP may fall, pulse pressure may narrow, heart rate may rise, urine output may decrease and lactate may rise. The arterial waveform may show reduced stroke volume. If the patient is fluid responsive and fluid tolerant, volume replacement may improve stroke volume and MAP. However, after cardiac surgery the cause of volume loss matters: ongoing surgical bleeding, coagulopathy, drain blockage and tamponade require different actions.

In haemorrhage, preload management is not just crystalloid administration. The nurse must track chest drain output, haemoglobin trend, coagulation results, temperature, calcium, acid-base status and haemodynamic response. Excess crystalloid can worsen haemodilution, tissue oedema and pulmonary mechanics. Blood products and surgical review may be required depending on bleeding volume, trend and local protocol.
Preload in vasoplegia

In vasoplegia, the vascular system is dilated. Venous capacitance increases and stressed volume decreases. The patient may have low MAP, low SVR, warm peripheries, low diastolic pressure and normal or high cardiac output. Effective venous return may still be inadequate if venous tone is very low. Fluid may temporarily improve filling, but vasopressor support is often needed to restore vascular tone and recruit stressed volume.

The risk is over-resuscitating the vasoplegic patient with fluid when the main problem is vascular tone. This can produce tissue oedema, pulmonary oedema, impaired oxygenation and delayed recovery. The better question is whether a measured fluid challenge improves stroke volume or whether vasopressor optimisation is the more physiological intervention.
Fluid responsiveness versus fluid tolerance

Fluid responsiveness means that stroke volume or cardiac output increases after a preload challenge. A commonly used threshold is an increase in stroke volume or cardiac output of around 10 to 15 percent. This tells you that the heart is operating on the preload-responsive part of the Starling curve at that moment. It does not prove that the patient should receive unlimited fluid.

Fluid tolerance means the patient can accept extra volume without harmful consequences. A patient with RV failure may be responsive to a small bolus but intolerant because the extra volume worsens RV dilation and venous congestion. A patient with a stiff LV may show a small cardiac output increase but develop pulmonary oedema from a large rise in LVEDP or PAWP. A patient with poor oxygenation may not tolerate fluid-induced lung water.

A good fluid response is not simply a higher CVP. A good response is improved stroke volume, cardiac index, pulse pressure, MAP with less vasopressor, SvO2, lactate trajectory, urine output or skin perfusion, with only an acceptable rise in filling pressures and no worsening oxygenation. A poor response is a rise in CVP or PAWP without improved flow. A harmful response is worse oxygenation, falling lung compliance, RV dilation, rising venous congestion or worsening urine output.
Dynamic preload assessment

Dynamic preload assessment tries to predict whether stroke volume will rise if preload is increased. Methods include passive leg raise, stroke volume variation, pulse pressure variation, end-expiratory occlusion testing, mini-fluid challenge and echo-based assessment. These methods are useful only when their assumptions are valid.

Pulse pressure variation and stroke volume variation are most reliable in fully mechanically ventilated patients with regular rhythm, sufficient tidal volume, closed chest conditions and no major RV failure. They become unreliable with atrial fibrillation, frequent ectopy, spontaneous breathing, low tidal volume ventilation, open chest, poor arterial waveform quality, high respiratory effort, severe RV dysfunction or major changes in vascular tone. In CT-ICU, many patients violate these assumptions, so dynamic indices must be interpreted cautiously.
Practical preload decision sequence

content.title: Bedside preload interpretation sequence
items: Confirm measurement reliability. Check CVP or PAWP transducer level, zeroing, waveform quality, catheter source, respiratory timing and patient position.
Identify the clinical problem. Decide whether the issue is low MAP, narrow pulse pressure, low cardiac index, rising lactate, falling SvO2, oliguria, pulmonary oedema or rising CVP.
Assess right-sided pressure. Review CVP trend, CVP waveform if visible, RV size on echo, PA pressures if present, oxygenation, PaCO2, pH and PEEP.
Assess left-sided filling. Review PAWP if valid, oxygenation, lung compliance, crackles, CXR pattern, echo LV size and signs of pulmonary congestion.

Assess whether the patient is likely to be fluid responsive. Use previous fluid response, passive leg raise if appropriate, stroke volume trend, pulse pressure response, echo and dynamic indices only if valid.

Assess whether the patient is fluid tolerant. Look for high CVP, high PAWP, RV dilation, septal shift, pulmonary oedema, worsening oxygenation, high PA pressures, renal venous congestion and liver congestion.
If fluid is given, define the endpoint before starting. Look for improved stroke volume, cardiac index, pulse pressure, MAP, SvO2, urine output or lactate trajectory.
Stop fluid if filling pressures rise without flow improvement or if oxygenation, RV function or venous congestion worsens.
Escalate urgently when falling MAP is paired with rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure, oliguria or rising lactate after cardiac surgery.

content.title: Preload interpretation map
content.description: A flowchart separating preload deficiency, poor fluid tolerance, RV failure, tamponade and vasoplegia using CVP, PAWP, pulse pressure, cardiac output and clinical perfusion.
elements: Start with low MAP, low CI or poor perfusion
Assess pulse pressure and cardiac index
Assess PAWP or echo LV filling if available
Check drains, haemoglobin and fluid balance
Check RV size, PA pressures, PEEP, oxygenation, PaCO2 and pH
Classify as underfilled, vasoplegic, RV failure, tamponade, LV congestion or mixed
teachingPoints: Fluid responsiveness and fluid tolerance are different.
Rising CVP with falling output after cardiac surgery is a high-risk pattern.

content.text: Clinical Pearls

content.title: Pearl 1: CVP is not a tank gauge
content.text: CVP is right-sided pressure. It is affected by RV function, PVR, PEEP, intrathoracic pressure, tamponade, tricuspid regurgitation, venous tone and compliance. It does not directly measure circulating volume. Treating CVP alone as a fluid instruction is unsafe.

content.title: Pearl 2: LVEDP depends on compliance
content.text: A stiff LV can have a high LVEDP with modest volume. This is common after aortic stenosis, LV hypertrophy, hypertension, ischaemia and bypass-related myocardial oedema. Extra fluid may raise pulmonary venous pressure and cause pulmonary oedema without improving stroke volume.

content.title: Pearl 3: fluid responsiveness is not fluid tolerance
content.text: A patient can increase stroke volume after a small preload challenge but still be harmed by the volume. This is common in RV failure, pulmonary hypertension and stiff LV physiology. Always look for congestion harm as well as flow benefit.

content.title: Pearl 4: rising CVP with shock after cardiac surgery is dangerous
content.text: Falling MAP with rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure, oliguria and rising lactate should trigger concern for tamponade, RV failure, high PVR, excessive PEEP or tension physiology. This pattern needs urgent escalation and mechanical assessment, not reflex fluid loading.

content.title: Pearl 5: RV failure can cause high CVP and low LV preload at the same time
content.text: When the RV cannot eject through the pulmonary circulation, blood backs up into the venous system and less blood reaches the LV. The CVP rises while LV filling and systemic cardiac output fall. More fluid may worsen RV dilation and septal shift.

content.title: Pearl 6: PEEP changes preload interpretation
content.text: Positive pressure and PEEP can raise measured CVP while reducing venous return and changing transmural filling pressure. Ventilator settings are part of haemodynamic interpretation, especially in RV-risk patients.

content.title: Pearl 7: low drain output does not exclude tamponade
content.text: A blocked or clotted mediastinal drain can give falsely reassuring low output while blood accumulates around the heart. Haemodynamic pattern matters: rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure, falling MAP and rising lactate are more important than a single drain number.

content.title: Pearl 8: venous tone is part of preload
content.text: After bypass, vasoplegia can reduce stressed volume and venous return even when the patient has received significant fluid. Vasopressors may improve preload physiology by recruiting venous capacitance, not just by raising arterial pressure.

content.title: Pearl 9: a good fluid bolus improves flow, not just pressure
content.text: The useful endpoint is improved stroke volume, cardiac index, pulse pressure, SvO2, urine output or lactate trend. A rise in CVP alone is not a success. A rise in CVP with worse oxygenation or no flow improvement is a warning.
Equipment Tie-In
CVP transducer setup and interpretation

CVP accuracy depends on correct transducer setup. The transducer should be levelled to the phlebostatic axis or local reference point, zeroed to atmospheric pressure according to unit procedure and checked for waveform quality. If the transducer is too low, the pressure reads falsely high. If it is too high, the pressure reads falsely low. Air bubbles, clot, loose connections, catheter whip or occlusion can distort the waveform.

The CVP number should be interpreted with respiratory phase, ventilation mode, PEEP and patient position. In mechanically ventilated patients, pressures may vary with positive pressure breaths. Trends are usually more useful than isolated values. A sudden rise in CVP with falling MAP and narrowing pulse pressure is more clinically meaningful than a chronically elevated but stable CVP in a known pulmonary hypertension patient.
CVP waveform

The CVP waveform can provide rhythm and right-heart information. The a wave reflects atrial contraction and disappears in AF. Large a waves can occur when the right atrium contracts against a stiff RV or closed tricuspid valve timing. The c wave relates to tricuspid closure and early RV contraction. The v wave reflects venous filling of the right atrium while the tricuspid valve is closed. Detailed CVP waveform interpretation belongs in Domain 2, but preload interpretation improves when the nurse understands that the waveform reflects events, not just a number.
Pulmonary artery catheter and PAWP

The pulmonary artery catheter can provide PA pressures, PAWP, cardiac output, cardiac index, SVR, PVR and SvO2. PAWP is used as a left-sided filling pressure estimate only when the wedge is valid. A proper wedge waveform should be recognised and the balloon should not be left inflated. Overwedging can risk pulmonary artery injury. Underwedging or catheter migration gives misleading values.

PAWP should be read with the patient's respiratory phase and ventilator settings. High PEEP can influence measured pressures. If PAWP does not fit oxygenation, echo or clinical signs, it should be questioned. In CT-ICU, PAWP is most valuable when combined with cardiac index, CVP, PA pressure, SvO2 and echo.
Arterial line response to preload

The arterial line can show response to preload changes. If a fluid challenge improves stroke volume, pulse pressure often widens and the systolic upstroke may become stronger. If pulse pressure does not improve and CVP rises, fluid is probably not improving forward flow. However, arterial line damping must be excluded before interpreting pulse pressure response.
Cardiac output monitors

Cardiac output monitors can quantify whether preload changes improve stroke volume or cardiac output. A 10 to 15 percent increase in stroke volume or cardiac output after a preload challenge suggests fluid responsiveness. Reliability depends on the monitor type, arterial waveform quality, rhythm, calibration, vascular tone and clinical conditions.
Echo

Echo is the most useful tool when preload numbers conflict. It can show a small underfilled LV, a dilated failing RV, septal shift, tamponade, LV systolic dysfunction, valve obstruction, severe regurgitation and gross volume state. It can also show whether fluid is likely to worsen RV dilation or pulmonary congestion.
Ventilator

The ventilator influences preload. PEEP and inspiratory pressure affect venous return, right-heart loading and measured filling pressures. A change in PEEP can change CVP and cardiac output without any change in intravascular volume. In RV-risk patients, ventilation and preload must be interpreted together.

content.title: Equipment problems that can mislead preload interpretation
rows: False high or false low CVP
Re-level to reference point and rezero if needed
Inspect tubing, aspirate/flush according to policy, check waveform
False wedge pressure and risk of harm
Check waveform, balloon volume, catheter position and operator technique
Fast flush/square wave test and tubing inspection
High PEEP or high intrathoracic pressure
Raised measured filling pressures and reduced venous return
Review PEEP, plateau pressure, driving pressure and haemodynamic response
Check rhythm, waveform quality and monitor limitations

content.title: CVP is pressure, not volume
content.description: A comparison diagram showing different clinical states that can all produce a high CVP.
teachingPoints: The same CVP number can mean several different mechanisms.
Treatment depends on the pattern, not the pressure alone.
High CVP with low output is a warning pattern after cardiac surgery.

content.text: Targets and Numbers

Preload targets are contextual. The target is not a specific CVP or PAWP for every patient. The target is adequate stroke volume and oxygen delivery without harmful filling pressures or congestion. A useful preload state is one where the ventricle can fill enough to eject effectively, but not so much that pulmonary oedema, RV dilation, hepatic congestion or renal venous congestion worsen.

content.title: Preload-related values
rows: Right-sided filling pressure and venous congestion clue
Not a volume gauge; affected by RV failure, PEEP, PVR, tamponade and compliance
Often 8 to 15 mmHg if waveform is valid
Can be misleading with invalid wedge, high PEEP, mitral disease or poor catheter position
Often around 5 to 12 mmHg in normal physiology, but variable in disease
High in stiff LV or diastolic dysfunction even without massive volume
SV or CO increase around 10 to 15 percent after preload challenge
Suggests the ventricle is on the responsive part of the Frank-Starling curve
No fixed number; judged by absence of harmful congestion
Determines whether extra volume is safe
Poor tolerance shown by rising CVP/PAWP, pulmonary oedema, RV dilation or worsening oxygenation
Low output may reflect low flow, AKI, venous congestion or obstruction
Rising lactate despite fluid suggests flow has not improved or demand remains high
Usually 60 to 80 percent; often target at least 65 to 70 percent if context agrees
Low value after fluid suggests delivery still inadequate or demand high

content.title: Preload patterns and likely mechanisms
rows: Drain output, falling Hb, tachycardia, low filling pressures
Falling MAP, oliguria, rising lactate, possible low drain output if blocked
RV dilation, high PA pressure, septal shift, hypoxaemia or acidosis
Right heart cannot eject; LV underfilled
Ventilator change, high airway pressures, sudden hypotension, hypoxaemia
Reduced venous return and increased RV afterload
Warm, low diastolic pressure, low SVR, normal/high CI
Reduced vascular tone and stressed volume
High PAWP, pulmonary oedema, poor compliance, hypoxaemia
High filling pressure with limited useful preload reserve

content.title: Fluid challenge interpretation
rows: SV or CO rises at least 10 to 15 percent
No meaningful SV or CO rise
Flat Starling response or wrong problem
No flow gain or worsening flow
Rising CVP/PAWP with worse oxygenation or RV dilation
Ongoing bleeding, vasoplegia, RV failure or unresolved obstruction possible
Look for cause rather than repeating boluses blindly

content.title: Escalation triggers for preload-related deterioration
items: Falling MAP with rising CVP and narrowing pulse pressure after cardiac surgery.
Rising CVP with falling cardiac index, oliguria, rising lactate or falling SvO2.
High CVP with RV dilation, septal shift, worsening oxygenation, hypercapnia or acidosis.
High PAWP with worsening pulmonary oedema, hypoxaemia or falling lung compliance.
Ongoing bleeding with falling Hb, increasing drain output, tachycardia or falling filling pressures.
Low drain output with worsening haemodynamics and rising CVP, suggesting possible drain blockage and tamponade.
No improvement in stroke volume or cardiac output after a preload challenge despite rising filling pressures.
Sudden haemodynamic deterioration after ventilator or PEEP changes.

quiz.question: What does preload most accurately describe?
options: Myocardial fibre stretch at end-diastole before contraction.
The total amount of water in the body.
The systolic blood pressure.
The oxygen saturation of venous blood.
quiz.explanation: Preload is the stretch on myocardial fibres at the end of diastole. It is related to ventricular filling, but bedside measures such as CVP and PAWP are pressure estimates and not direct measurements of total volume.

quiz.question: Which statement about CVP is safest?
options: CVP is a right-sided pressure estimate and must be interpreted with RV function, PEEP, PVR and the clinical picture.
CVP directly measures total circulating volume.
A low CVP always means the patient needs fluid.
A high CVP always means the patient is overfilled.
quiz.explanation: CVP is a pressure, not a tank gauge. It is affected by RV function, pulmonary vascular resistance, intrathoracic pressure, PEEP, tamponade, tricuspid regurgitation and compliance. It must be interpreted as part of a pattern.

quiz.question: A fresh post-cardiac surgery patient has falling MAP, rising CVP, narrowing pulse pressure, oliguria and rising lactate. What is the safest interpretation?
options: This is a high-risk pattern for tamponade, RV failure, high PVR, excessive PEEP or tension physiology.
This always proves simple hypovolaemia.
This proves the patient needs repeated large crystalloid boluses.
This is reassuring because CVP is high.
quiz.explanation: Falling MAP with rising CVP and narrowing pulse pressure suggests impaired filling or forward flow despite high right-sided pressure. After cardiac surgery, tamponade and RV failure must be considered urgently. Excess fluid may worsen the situation if the heart is compressed or the RV is failing.

quiz.question: A fluid bolus increases CVP from 12 to 17 mmHg, but cardiac index and pulse pressure do not improve and oxygenation worsens. What does this suggest?
options: The patient is not deriving useful flow benefit and is showing poor fluid tolerance.
The bolus was successful because CVP increased.
The patient definitely needs more fluid until CVP is above 20.
The arterial line must be underdamped.
quiz.explanation: A useful fluid response should improve flow or perfusion markers. A rise in CVP without improved cardiac index or pulse pressure, especially with worse oxygenation, suggests pressure without flow benefit and poor fluid tolerance.

quiz.question: Why can RV failure cause high CVP but low systemic cardiac output?
options: The RV cannot eject forward, so venous pressure rises while less blood reaches the LV.
High CVP always means LV preload is high.
The LV automatically increases stroke volume when the RV fails.
RV failure directly lowers systemic vascular resistance to zero.
quiz.explanation: In RV failure, blood backs up into the venous system, raising CVP. At the same time, the RV delivers less blood through the pulmonary circulation, so LV preload falls. Systemic cardiac output may drop despite high right-sided pressure.

quiz.question: Why can high PEEP make preload interpretation difficult?
options: It can raise measured intrathoracic filling pressures while reducing venous return and altering transmural pressure.
It directly measures LVEDP.
It always increases stroke volume.
It makes CVP equal to total blood volume.
quiz.explanation: High PEEP increases intrathoracic pressure, which can raise measured CVP and affect venous return. It can also alter RV afterload depending on lung volume and pulmonary vascular effects. Therefore CVP must be interpreted with ventilator settings and clinical response.

diagrams.title: Preload interpretation algorithm
diagrams.description: A bedside decision pathway for interpreting CVP, PAWP, pulse pressure, cardiac output and clinical perfusion in CT-ICU.
elements: Start with hypotension, low CI, oliguria, rising lactate or falling SvO2
Validate CVP and arterial line setup
Assess pulse pressure and cardiac index
Assess PAWP or LV filling if available
Assess RV size, PA pressure, PEEP, oxygenation, PaCO2 and pH
Classify as preload deficiency, vasoplegia, RV failure, tamponade, LV congestion or mixed pattern
teachingPoints: A CVP number alone is never enough.
Fluid should improve flow, not just filling pressure.
Rising CVP with falling output after cardiac surgery is a danger pattern.

diagrams.title: CVP is pressure, not volume
diagrams.description: A comparison diagram showing several mechanisms that can produce the same high CVP value.
teachingPoints: High CVP can coexist with low LV preload.

diagrams.title: Fluid responsiveness versus fluid tolerance
diagrams.description: A two-axis diagram showing whether a patient increases stroke volume after fluid and whether the patient develops congestion harm.
elements: Responsive and tolerant: flow improves, pressure rise acceptable
Responsive but intolerant: flow improves but congestion worsens
Not responsive but tolerant: little flow gain, no immediate harm
Not responsive and intolerant: no flow gain, pressure and congestion worsen
teachingPoints: Responsiveness is about stroke volume increase.
A good fluid decision requires both concepts.

references.title: Physiology, Cardiac Preload
references.supports: Supports preload definition as ventricular stretch at end-diastole and its relationship to stroke volume through Frank-Starling physiology.

references.title: Physiology, Central Venous Pressure
references.supports: Supports central venous pressure physiology, measurement and interpretation as right atrial pressure rather than direct blood volume.

references.title: Central venous pressure: we need to bring clinical use into physiological context
references.supports: Supports interpreting CVP in physiological context rather than using it as an isolated volume marker.

references.title: Does central venous pressure predict fluid responsiveness? A systematic review of the literature and the tale of seven mares
references.supports: Supports the limitation that CVP alone poorly predicts fluid responsiveness.

references.title: Prediction of fluid responsiveness: an update
references.supports: Supports dynamic assessment of fluid responsiveness and limitations of static preload markers.

references.title: Fluid responsiveness and the six guiding principles of fluid resuscitation
references.supports: Supports fluid responsiveness as a physiological response and the need to consider fluid tolerance and harm.

references.title: Right ventricular failure in pulmonary hypertension: diagnosis and new perspectives on vascular and direct right ventricular treatment
references.supports: Supports RV failure physiology, including high right-sided pressures, RV-pulmonary vascular interaction and systemic consequences.

references.title: Cardiac Tamponade: A Clinical Challenge
references.supports: Supports tamponade physiology as impaired cardiac filling due to pericardial pressure and its haemodynamic consequences.
